sheet.label: DATA & DEPLOYMENT
sheet.title: 9. Operational Dashboard & Analytics
sheet.p: While the chatbot can operate as a standalone lightweight application, organizations often require visibility into system usage, AI performance, operational costs, and user interactions. To address these needs, an optional Operational Dashboard is proposed.

The dashboard provides centralized monitoring and analytics, enabling administrators to understand how the chatbot is performing, identify knowledge gaps, monitor API usage, and make informed decisions about future improvements.
The dashboard is optional and is recommended for production deployments where long-term monitoring and business insights are required.
When the dashboard is enabled, PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension is recommended as the primary database.
The database stores both vector embeddings and operational data, allowing semantic search and analytics to coexist within a single platform.
This architecture reduces infrastructure complexity by avoiding multiple databases for related operational data.
The Operational Dashboard is organized into multiple functional modules.
This section provides insights into chatbot usage.
These metrics help evaluate chatbot adoption and user engagement.
This module measures how effectively the knowledge base is serving user queries.
These insights help identify areas where website content may need improvement.
This module monitors LLM usage and operational cost.
Monitoring these metrics helps optimize AI usage and control operational expenses.
If a feedback mechanism is enabled, administrators can analyze user satisfaction.
This information supports continuous improvement of chatbot quality.
This module provides operational visibility into the application.
These metrics help administrators detect issues before they impact users.
When PostgreSQL is used, the following logical tables are recommended.
API request statistics and token usage
Additional tables can be introduced as future requirements evolve.
The dashboard homepage can present a high-level overview of system performance.
Percentage of queries answered using indexed content
Timestamp of the latest knowledge update
These KPIs allow administrators to assess chatbot performance at a glance.
The Operational Dashboard provides several business and technical benefits.
The modular dashboard architecture allows future expansion without redesign.
The dashboard is considered an optional enterprise feature.

Organizations that require only chatbot functionality can deploy the lightweight architecture without operational analytics. As business requirements evolve, the enterprise architecture can be adopted without changing the overall chatbot design.
Supports both semantic search and operational analytics within a single database platform.
Allows independent enhancement of analytics features.
Simplifies monitoring by organizing metrics into functional categories.
Provides quick visibility into chatbot health and business performance.
Keeps the MVP lightweight while enabling enterprise-grade observability when required.

sheet.label: DATA & DEPLOYMENT
sheet.title: 10. Security & Privacy
sheet.p: Although the Theta Chat Bot is publicly accessible and does not require user authentication, security remains a critical aspect of the overall system design. The chatbot communicates with external AI services, processes user input, and retrieves information from the company's knowledge base. Therefore, appropriate security controls must be implemented to ensure system reliability, protect sensitive configurations, and prevent misuse.
The proposed security strategy follows a defense-in-depth approach, where multiple layers of protection work together to reduce potential risks.
The primary security objectives of the chatbot are:
Protect backend services from unauthorized access.
Secure communication between all system components.
Maintain the integrity of the knowledge base.
Ensure reliable and uninterrupted chatbot operation.
Since the chatbot answers questions only about Theta Technolabs, it is intentionally restricted to information available in the approved knowledge base.
All communication between the website, backend, vector database, and external AI provider should use encrypted channels.
TLS encryption for external API requests.
Secure communication between internal services where applicable.
Automatic redirection from HTTP to HTTPS.
Encrypted communication protects user requests from interception and helps maintain data integrity.
Because the chatbot APIs are publicly accessible, they should include multiple validation mechanisms.
These controls help reduce misuse while ensuring consistent API behavior.
The chatbot communicates with external AI providers such as Google Gemini and, potentially, OpenAI.
API credentials must never be exposed to the frontend.
Store API keys in environment variables.
Never hardcode credentials in source code.
Restrict API key permissions where supported.
Maintain separate credentials for development, testing, and production environments.
All AI requests should originate from the backend to ensure credentials remain protected.
The chatbot should retrieve information only from the approved Theta Technolabs knowledge base.
Only indexed website content should be used.
Unauthorized data sources should not be queried.
Content updates should follow the synchronization pipeline.
Metadata should be preserved for traceability.
Version information should be maintained for indexed content.
These practices help ensure that chatbot responses remain accurate and aligned with official company information.
The chatbot is designed to answer general questions about Theta Technolabs and does not require users to register or provide personal information.
No personally identifiable information (PII) is collected intentionally.
Temporary session identifiers are used only to maintain conversational context during the active browser session.
Session data should expire automatically after a configurable period of inactivity.
If future business requirements introduce features such as inquiry forms or CRM integration, privacy requirements should be reviewed and updated accordingly.
Application logging is important for troubleshooting, monitoring, and operational analysis.
Sensitive information such as API keys, authentication tokens, or confidential configuration values must never be written to application logs.
Restrict responses to retrieved knowledge base content and enforce system prompt rules.
Implement rate limiting and request validation.
Expose only required public endpoints and validate all requests.
Store credentials securely on the server using environment variables.
Sanitize and validate all user input before processing.
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with confidence-based response validation.
Return a user-friendly fallback message and log the incident for investigation.
As the chatbot evolves, additional security features may be introduced.
Role-based access control for the operational dashboard
Secrets management using a vault solution
These enhancements can be adopted incrementally as operational requirements grow.
Protects data in transit and ensures secure communication.
Prevents exposure of AI provider credentials to the client.
Improves security and simplifies deployment across environments.
Reduces hallucinations by grounding responses in approved website content.
Supports conversational continuity without requiring user authentication.
Allows the security model to evolve alongside future business requirements.

sheet.label: DATA & DEPLOYMENT
sheet.title: 11. Deployment Strategy
sheet.p: The chatbot is containerized using Docker for consistent, portable deployment across development, testing, and production. Initial deployment is on-premise, hosted within company infrastructure — giving full control, predictable costs, and easier compliance. The architecture remains cloud-ready for future migration to AWS, Azure, or GCP with minimal changes.
EnvironmentPurposeDevelopmentFeature development and local testingTesting / UATValidation before production releaseProductionLive deployment for website visitors

ContainerResponsibilityNginxReverse proxy, SSL termination, routingFastAPIREST APIs and chatbot business logicQdrant (Lightweight)Vector storage and semantic searchPostgreSQL + pgvector (Enterprise)Vector storage, analytics, operational datapgAdmin (Optional)PostgreSQL administrationMonitoring Stack (Future)Metrics collection and visualization
Option A – Lightweight: FastAPI + Qdrant + Nginx + Docker. Minimal resources, fast setup, low maintenance.
Option B – Enterprise: Adds PostgreSQL + pgvector + pgAdmin for conversation analytics, token/cost tracking, feedback analysis, and an operational dashboard.

All configuration (API keys, DB strings, LLM provider, ports, logging, session timeout) is externalized via environment variables — never hardcoded. Backups run on a schedule for PostgreSQL/Qdrant data, config files, and source code, with periodic restore testing. Since the knowledge base is derived from the live website, embeddings can be regenerated if lost.
Pull latest code → update config → build Docker images → start containers → verify DB and AI provider connectivity → run health checks → sync knowledge base → validate functionality → release.

DecisionJustificationDocker-based deploymentConsistency and portability across environmentsOn-premise hostingInfrastructure control and long-term cost efficiencyEnvironment-based configKeeps secrets out of the codebaseDual deployment architectureSupports lightweight and enterprise needs without redesign
